label: Lagrange Multiplier
definition: A method for optimizing under constraints by adding one new variable per constraint, converting a constrained problem into an unconstrained stationarity condition. Its deepest meaning is that each multiplier is a price: the rate at which the best achievable objective would improve if that constraint were loosened, which is why it is the foundation of duality.

body.text: A Lagrange multiplier turns a constrained optimization problem into an unconstrained one, and the geometry behind it is a single clean coincidence. Suppose you want to minimize a function while obeying an equality constraint that confines you to a surface. At the constrained optimum you cannot move in any allowed direction and improve, which forces the gradient of the objective to point in the same direction as the gradient of the constraint, since any component of the objective's gradient lying along the surface would let you descend further. The multiplier is the scalar expressing that proportionality, and Lagrange's insight was to fold it into one combined function, the Lagrangian, formed as the objective minus the multiplier times the constraint, whose ordinary stationary points recover exactly the constrained optima together with the constraints themselves.

Mechanically, you introduce one multiplier per constraint, build the Lagrangian, and set all its partial derivatives to zero. The derivatives with respect to the original variables impose the gradient-alignment condition; the derivatives with respect to the multipliers reproduce the constraints. The result is a single system of equations encoding both stationarity and feasibility, solvable by the same calculus as any unconstrained problem. For inequality constraints the method generalizes to the Karush-Kuhn-Tucker conditions, which add that each multiplier be nonnegative and be zero whenever its constraint is slack, the complementary-slackness rule that only the constraints actively pressing on the solution carry a nonzero price.

That word price is the deepest interpretation, and it is what makes the multiplier far more than a trick for solving. A Lagrange multiplier measures the sensitivity of the optimal value to its constraint: it is the rate at which the best achievable objective would improve if the constraint were loosened by one unit, a shadow price in the language of economics. A binding constraint with a large multiplier is costing you dearly; a constraint with a zero multiplier is not limiting you at all. This reading is the foundation of duality in convex optimization, where the multipliers become the dual variables and the dual problem is built entirely from them.

In machine learning the method is pervasive, often beneath the surface. The dual of the support vector machine introduces a multiplier per training example, and the optimal multipliers are nonzero only for the support vectors on the margin, which is both why the solution is sparse and why the kernel trick applies. Regularization has a multiplier reading too: training subject to a bound on a model's norm is equivalent, through a multiplier, to adding a penalty term, the formal link between constrained capacity and weight decay. Deriving the maximum-entropy distribution under moment constraints, which yields the Gaussian, the exponential, and the softmax, is a Lagrange-multiplier calculation, and the entropy-regularized optimal transport solved by the Sinkhorn algorithm attaches multipliers to the marginal constraints, where they are exactly the Kantorovich potentials. The unifying message is that constraints are not obstacles to be worked around but quantities with prices, and the multiplier both enforces a constraint and tells you what it costs.

image.alt: Concentric dashed level curves of an objective function f, each with small gradient arrows pointing inward, crossed by a red constraint curve g equals c; the red and dashed curves are tangent where the constrained optimum sits.
image.caption: The geometry behind Lagrange multipliers. The dashed curves are level sets of the objective f, and the red curve is the constraint g equals c. At the constrained optimum the constraint curve is tangent to a level curve, so the two gradients line up; the multiplier is the scalar that expresses how much one gradient must be scaled to equal the other. Where the curves merely cross, you can still slide along the constraint to a better value.
